author: @RedRake
text: Do you think graphite lubricants are of high enough quality for exfoliation into graphene? There's this graphite lubricant product, about $2 for 3 grams, and so based on the price I'm guessing it might be high quality. Not quite sure though.

replies.author: @RedRake
replies.text: +Robert Murray-Smith just used it to make a transparent flexible conductive sheet using your method and it works amazingly. the graphite flakes are quite large - I'd say about 0.5 square mm on average. no wonder it's so expensive compared to other graphite lubricants.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that's very cool mate - do you have a resistance figure?

replies.author: @RedRake
replies.text: +Robert Murray-Smith about 3 kilohms with the probes about an inch apart - I don't know how to do square resistance. It could be better since I was just playing around with this, using only a paper towel and the graphite powder and rubbing it onto a random piece of plastic.

replies.author: @jfrjr7964
replies.text: +Robert Murray-Smith isn't Graphene one carbon atom layer? If you have acetone, which also is a carbon base chemical you will be contaminating your product with different chemical structure. Graphite has a crystalline structure which is closer to Graphene, than other chemicals. In my opinion, you will need to use a product which you will be able to remove later with minimal left over by product.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +JFR Jr fair enough

author: @jeffyung8511
text: density of acetone should be 0.791 g/ml  ,  of water is 1 g/ml .    So  if to  keep  the  mass  ratio  3 to 1 ,  the  volume  ratio  of  acetone  to  water  should  be  around  3.8  to  1.     But  according  to  the  list  you  use  ,  416.75ml  of  acetone  and  88.25ml  of  water ,  that   ratio  will  be   4.7  to  1 .     Is  my  calculation   correct?     Do  I  have  any  misunderstanding  about  the  ratio?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +Jeff Yung you are quite right

replies.author: @jeffyung8511
replies.text: +Robert Murray-Smith      Thanks  for  your  reply !     I  saw   another  your  video   using   industrial  top  down  blender  to  shear  exfoliate  graphite  to  be  graphene .    But  I  have  a  question .    For  sonication ,  you  can  put  on  the  cap  to  prevent   acetone   vaporize .    But   in  top  down  blender   method  ,  if  we  use  cosolvent  of  acetone/water ,  there  is  no  way  to  cap  the  bottle .     If  using  NMP  which   volatility  point  is  high ,  maybe  no  problem  at  all   as  long  as    stirring  time  is  not  too  long .  But   volatility  point  of  acetone  is  low ,   will   acetone    vaporize   too  much  after   2  hours   stirring ?    Looking  forward  to  your   suggestion .

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +Jeff Yung I have no suggestions

author: @electro1622
text: Just a tip about your ultrasonic...If you fill the unit up to the recommended level and then suspend your jar 1 inch below the surface, this will give you the optimum ultrasonic effect. Placing the jar on the bottom will work, but the action is greatly reduced. This may have a dramatic affect on the end quality of the graphene..?? Ultrasonics work on the principal of cavitation implosion, so a longer time may be what is breaking down the graphene sheets. An improved action may allow less breakdown because of the shorter time..???

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: electro1622 thank you for the tip

author: @AlanFrance21
text: A little 'off topic' , but I tried the 75% acetone/deionised.water mix for cleaning brushes I'd used for a lacquer called Rustin's 'Plastic Coating'.  It's a two-pot, hard, crystal-clear melamine resin lacquer I use a lot in my shop for really durable wooden floors, worktops and tables. author: @Slider2732
text:
Ah, I used store bought bottled water (got fluoride in the tap water) and it settled in a few hours.
But, maybe a tip or maybe not for a tiny test batch - I used a Dremmel and the metal 'chimney sweep brush' attachment in a pills bottle. The mix spun about for half an hour and then turned a very smooth silver, with less froth. Did another half hour and the bubbling became much finer. Seems the mix changes in stages, at least to the eye...though mine settled eventually to a black ink goo.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: test the goo!!!

author: @BinjKomisar11
text: Hello R M Smith. So, in the end of the process (lets say you ran it for 4 hours) you are left with a colloidal solution of high quality graphene and graphite. Can you explain to me how to isolate the  graphene out from the rest of the solution? Thanks a million, keep up the good work. B

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: centrifuge it at 1,000 rpm. pour of the liquid and keep it - the graphene is in the liquid. You are best off keeping the graphene in liquid until you are ready to use it.

replies.author: @BinjKomisar11
replies.text: Robert Murray-Smith Awesome. I'm looking forward to making some heavy-duty cap. Thanks for the info.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
Binj Komisar
go for it mate

author: @scianiac
text: You've mentioned before about separating the graphite and graphene with a centrifuge after the actual graphene exfoliation process. Have you seen any other effective ways of separating it, like by just letting it settle? Also I've looked around but haven't seen any exact centrifuge settings to do this. I've seen 4000 RPM on a 14cm radius rotor for separating GO before though.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: 1,000 rpm is recommended in the paper i quoted. You can just let it settle but there is no guarantee that you are getting 100% proof that way - though i think it works ok

author: @pozytywniezakrecony151
text: Great tutorial as always. Could you be so kind to also put hardware info in such videos? For example some very high level specs of blender/ sonic heater used :) Do you prefer blender method or this one (both with acetone)?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I prefer the shear mixer as it doesn't damage the graphene plates as much as sonication does. I do put machine specs in - but it is always a bit difficult to be too specific as people have a whole range of machinery already, they want to make their own or just can't buy the machine i use so it is of limited value - mostly the guidelines give you a place to start - if it doesn't work out the same you will have to tweak it for your own particular set up - but that is experimentation and that is what this channel is about

replies.author: @pozytywniezakrecony151
replies.text:
Robert Murray-Smith
Nice, blender is also cheaper for amateurs ;) Fingers crossed for your next experiments. Cheers

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
Piotr Radziszewski
lol - cheers mate - and good luck with yours too

replies.author: @pozytywniezakrecony151
replies.text: Robert Murray-Smith finally made it - was quiet afraid of acetone at start and to not blow up blender...I ended up mixing for around 12-15 minutes and then cooling it in cold water for around 5-10 minutes and repeating the process so the blender doesnt go too hot. I've added few drops of some decent German kitchen dishwashing detergent, which cleans everything nicely and also seems to dissolve graphitic powder. In the end I got some very dark suspension - nearly 1l in a jar :) At the moment running second and probably final batch for now - got to plan something to test on with it.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
Piotr Radziszewski
brilliant mate

author: @bernardstokes6550
text: just to finish off the previous comment perhaps a set of calibration weights for approx 7 pounds http://www.ebay.co.uk/itm/5PCS-50g-20g-10g-5g-Grams-Precision-Chrome-Calibration-Scale-Weight-Set-Kit-UK-/350728111886?pt=UK_BOI_Electrical_Test_Measurement_Equipment_ET&hash=item51a906470e   just to be sure.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: mate, you don't need to be that exact - 2 decimal places is fine at this volume. If you were doing 40mL and needed to add 10mg of graphite then your balance needs to be pretty accurate but at 500ml and 1.5g it's not so much a worry. You are going to lose as much material sticking to the weighing boat or the bottle transferring things as you are going to gain in accuracy from super accurate equipment.

author: @bernardstokes6550
text:
would not one of those rather inexpensive balances to two decimal places be a more straight forward way of measuring these sorts of quantities?
http://www.ebay.co.uk/itm/0-01-300g-Digital-Electronic-Balance-Weight-Scale-/270583494191?pt=UK_BOI_Electrical_Test_Measurement_Equipment_ET&hash=item3f0008762f  maybe of 	 	 	 	dubious quality but even if it were inaccurate to a factor of 10 it would still be more accurate than many other methods.......and of course there are many others of better quality.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: sure - why not - though i guess it is a 6 and 2 threes which ever way you go with

author: @JonathanFosdickNano
text: :) Been thinking of making a sonicator myself.  I recently found out that you can buy the 50W piezoelectric transducer replacement online for little over £11. And I have already came up with a simple schematic that will generate the 22.5kHz needed for graphene exfoliation and the parts for that (including 2 power MOSFETs) would cost about the same. I reckon a DIY tip sonicator is much cheaper than the ones they sell for thousands.  And with little modification, you could make a bath sonicator as well, and I am desperately needing one!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: You will definitely have to do a vid on that mate

replies.author: @JonathanFosdickNano
replies.text: Definately mate!  That would make a great DIY video :) I am thinking of getting my hands on a broken bread maker and using that for the frame and bath insert.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: nice - give me a heads up when you make it - i might be making one myself when the one I have  got breaks!
